topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the common room hummed with a strained, high-pitched whine, mirroring the tightness in Maya’s chest. She kept running a hand over the worn fabric of her hoodie, the rough texture grounding her slightly against the swirling uncertainty. Every time her phone buzzed with an unfamiliar email notification, her breath hitched. She stared at the printed memo spread across her desk—the official notice regarding the campus closure—and the words swam, refusing to settle into comprehension. Her fingers began to tap a frantic rhythm on the wooden surface, a restless energy that wouldn't subside. She needed a plan, a solid structure, but the very thought of looking forward felt like trying to walk through thick mud. She just wanted the noise in her head to stop, to quiet the relentless cataloging of worst-case scenarios.

[story 2]

Liam paced the length of his small dorm room three times before stopping abruptly, leaning his forehead against the cool plaster of the wall. The silence of the evening was oppressive, amplifying the distant sounds of campus activity—laughter, footsteps, the usual vibrant backdrop of college life that was now receding. He kept checking his email, the screen illuminating the pale sheen of sweat on his palms. Each time a new piece of information surfaced about the impending shutdown, a cold knot tightened deep in his stomach, making it hard to draw a full breath. He picked up a heavy textbook, flipping through pages he already knew by heart, needing the familiar weight to anchor him against the sudden, dizzying sense of instability washing over him.

[story 3]

"Are you sure this is real?" Sarah whispered, her voice thin, as she reread the final paragraph of the announcement. The air in the library felt suddenly too thick, suffocating. She kept glancing toward the main entrance, half-expecting someone to burst in and confirm the impossible news. Her hands trembled slightly as she set the paper down, the sound echoing loudly in the cavernous space. A wave of nausea washed over her, making her grip the edge of the table so tightly her knuckles turned white. She needed to move, to do something, but her legs felt weighted, rooted to the spot by a paralyzing fear of the unknown future.
